participants.extracted: Adults (age ≥18) with rheumatoid arthritis receiving care from one of eight rheumatologists in the Geisinger Rheumatology Department in central Pennsylvania, and currently being treated with at least one non-biologic DMARD, who had active disease warranting initiation or change of a biologic therapy as determined by their treating rheumatologist. Eligible patients were English-speaking and able to read English; patients already on a biologic were also eligible if they could be directed to relevant chapter content. Key exclusions included hearing or visual impairment, upcoming surgery, current infection, recent cancer (within 5 years, except non-melanoma skin cancer), history of lymphoma/leukemia/melanoma, other chronic inflammatory disease requiring immunosuppressive therapy, chronic hepatitis C or B liver disease, HIV positivity, and positive tuberculosis screening without adequate prophylaxis. Of 152 referred patients, 125 were randomized (61 control, 60 intervention with complete group counts reported in tables), and 117 were contacted for both follow-up surveys.

interventions.extracted: Participants randomized to the intervention arm received access to an interactive, web-based, computerized decision aid about rheumatoid arthritis biologic therapy. The tool, developed using fuzzy-trace theory and International Patient Decision Aid Standards principles, presented the benefits and risks of biologics in simple language with clear probabilities, graphics, optional voice-over, and a values-clarification task. Patients could access it at home or in clinic on a laptop, could use it over up to two weeks, and could revisit sections multiple times without restriction. If participants had not accessed the tool after five days, study staff called to offer assistance with access, after which they continued independently.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure multiple patient-centered outcomes related to decision making about biologic therapy in rheumatoid arthritis. At 2 weeks and again at 8 weeks, it assessed objective knowledge using a 20-item true/false questionnaire on expected benefits, risks, and route of administration of biologics (total score range 1–20). It measured subjective knowledge and clarity of values using two subscales from the Decisional Conflict Scale: a 3-item subjective knowledge subscale and a 3-item values clarity subscale, each scored on a 0–100 scale with lower scores indicating better knowledge/clarity; the combined score of these subscales was also used as a decisional conflict measure (0–100, lower scores indicating less conflict), with a predefined low-conflict threshold of 25 or less used to classify informed choice. The Combined Outcome Measure for Risk Communication (COMRADE) was administered at 2 and 8 weeks to assess risk communication and confidence in decision; it consists of 20 items on a 5-point agree scale and yields two 10-item subscales: risk communication and confidence in decision. In addition, the study measured whether participants made a decision to escalate care and whether actual escalation of care occurred by 8 weeks, as determined by chart review (biologic prescription/other treatment change). Health literacy was measured at baseline with a single-item confidence question about filling out medical forms on a 5-point scale, and disease activity was measured at baseline using the Clinical Disease Activity Index (CDAI). Feasibility outcomes also included the number of patients agreeing to participate and the number accessing the web-based tool, assessed during enrollment and the 2-week intervention period.
